content:
Member-only story

8 min read
Just now

--

A reproducible experiment that uncovered a missing layer in AI-readable design systems.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The first prototype implemented every principle as a visible feature. The corrected version resolved conflicts by prioritising one next action.I have been using Claude heavily for writing, product thinking, and design experiments. The more capable it became, the more often I noticed a strange mismatch. Claude could manage the complexity of the work, but I still had to manage the complexity of Claude.
Here is what that looks like in practice. I hand Claude a long research task and step away. When I come back, I get a wall of text. Somewhere in that wall is a question it asked me forty minutes ago. Somewhere else is a recommendation it later replaced with a better one, except both are still sitting there looking equally official. I scroll. I reconstruct. I do project management in my working memory, which is the one place it should never live.
Today’s AI workspaces already have permission systems, progress streams, activity logs, and long context windows. What they still struggle to provide is the one thing a returning user actually needs: a stable answer to “what should I do next?”
